As I see it, there are 3 major stages to maturity: (1) The child that listens to authority absolutely (2) The teenager that defies ...
... authority absolutely (3) The adult that thinks for themself. #3 listens to authority when it makes sense; the status quo is often OK.
Many never make it past #2 because cynicism and defiance give you pleasure at the expense of rationality and empathy.

As I add more and more custom-built productivity tools into my arsenal, I can't help but feel that automation of personal tasks is *still* very neglected.

Some things I've automated:

- Transcribe a short section of a podcast and upload it to my notes
- Index my @NotionHQ notes so I can randomly choose one
- Transforming clipboard text into various formats
- Back up computer to cloud storage
- Redeploy website on changes

Some things I haven't automated:

Scheduling video chats, creating flashcards for Chinese, moving data between notes apps, finding a therapist, making doctors' appointments...

There are products that do each of these things, but not all (except personal assistants)

You can automate work through apps like @zapier, @IFTTT, @parabolahq, Apple Shortcuts/Automator, @Wolfram_Alpha,  and defunct @yahoopipes.

But each app only supports a few dozen products at best. And almost nobody I know uses these apps anyway.

Why is that? Awareness? Learning curve? Fear of vendor lock-in? Hard to make or monetize?

Maybe potential users just learn to code instead.

Whoever makes a ubiquitous automation tool / virtual assistant will be my hero (+ very rich)... but only if my parents can figure it out.

No day is unsalvageable.

Reneging on promises to yourself (diet, sleep, exercise, ...) feels awful, but now you've got an opportunity to practice that "grit" thing all the cool kids are talking about.

What would a close friend say to cheer you up? Now imagine you are a close friend, because you are. Say it.

Be kind to this beautiful and vulnerable person. No one will ever know you as intimately as you do. That person should be your ally.

You can also structure an inner conflict as a dialog between 2 good friends / advocates for your wants and needs. They're both good listeners and are fighting for the same cause: your well-being.

(see Internal Double Crux https://t.co/34VxtCLBFE)

I'm going to share how I cite podcasts for my BASB notes (@NotionHQ) that eventually become my Zettelkasten permanent notes (@RoamResearch). Hope it inspires others.
1. When I hear something interesting on my podcast app (I use @pocketcasts), I grab a permalink for that specific time in that specific episode of that specific podcast. https://t.co/u3lnnG6BMS

2. I copy the permalink into a very ugly but functional web app I wrote, along with a description of what resonates with me.

Thanks @fortelabs for emphasizing "resonance".

Thanks @soenke_ahrens for emphasizing putting things into your own words. https://t.co/jCYBqkXq26

3. My web app downloads the podcast mp3 file along with the episode metadata (title, episode URL, publish date, etc).

The web app trims the mp3 file so it is only the 5 min leading up to that specific moment in the episode.
4. The web app uploads everything into my second brain (@NotionHQ) and schedules it to be transcribed by adding a reference under my "Transcription Queue" page. https://t.co/jTLfy2vW6q

5. In the past, I used the Google Cloud Speech-to-Text API for transcriptions, but they were pretty bad.

Now I've got a guy on @fiverr that does decent transcriptions. Every few days, I send him a batch of my mp3s and he gives me the transcriptions. This process is pretty fast.

6. My web app regularly indexes my second brain (@NotionHQ) so I can serendipitously visit random pages. Over some period of time, notes get progressively summarized.

https://t.co/j9Eh6GNPEp
https://t.co/WFHu0Yr2W9 https://t.co/G3ZeaYnFNh

7. When something really resonates, I create a Permanent Note in my Zettelkasten (@RoamResearch) and link back to the page in Notion.

I often pull in an analogous note here that has overlapping ideas.

Thanks @uberstuber for helping me bridge the PARA <> Zettelkasten gap. https://t.co/mF48vNl0Xl

Using the Infinite Improbability Drive to break out of the status quo and flourish.

https://t.co/sfdyHHoOtF

[thread]

We don't challenge core behaviors or beliefs very often. I may adopt a slightly healthier lifestyle or new hobby, but that's a small change.

If I switched to a new primary language, joined a gang, lost my hearing, broadened my sexuality, lived on a boat...

THAT would be CHANGE.

People say Chinese and English are very different. They say descendants from China and Europe can be very different.

But human DNA and culture vary insignificantly when you compare them to a dog's.

A European descendant is to a dog as English is to ??? THAT would me DIFFERENT.

Try to question the small AND the big in your life.

Pick any aspect and turn on the Infinite Improbability Drive.

Ask "What would happen if this was totally, TOTALLY different?"

Examples:
* I get car sick: Can I change that?
* I sleep on a bed: Is that necessary?
* I like mentoring youth: What won't they tell me because I'm not a peer?
* Problems with capitalism will be solved by money: Huh🤔?
* The US will be stable forever: What will I do if it's not?

Some will lead nowhere, most will lead somewhere. 

Being more systematic and intentional about change is the idea.

You are the blacksmith and the metal. You can apply as much heat as you want to shake up who you are 🔥.

(simulated annealing https://t.co/Rtc2JVeMQa)

It is through asking these questions that you will realize so many things about your life are arbitrary.

And anything that's arbitrary is a good candidate for change.

Instead of searching just the local space nearby, peek at who you could be if bigger things were different.

Has this ever happened to you?

You: I don't like X. It's too Y.
Them: Name one thing about X that is Y.
You: Uhh well...

Don't discount a gut feeling. The gut is lossy compression, but a useful tool.

1/4

Your gut cannot persuade others (except through demagoguery 😝).

Your gut can't undo adding an old belief when you discover it's wrong.

Your gut will make lots of mistakes.

But don't ignore it.

2/4

Low effort fixes

1. Track your gut's beliefs:

* When capturing your thoughts on paper/phone, add source "... via John Smith".
* Keep a diary with software that can be searched.

2. Is your gut talking or your energy level? When stating a belief, how's it feel in your body?

3/4

3. Qualify your gut beliefs:

You: My gut tells me I don't like X because it's too Y.
Them: Name one thing about X that is Y.
You: I can't. It's just my gut feeling.
Them: Um ok...

Like I said, not very persuasive. But it's informative nonetheless.

4/4

Use Tables 🪑 to collect and organize data for a specific part of your life.

Data Structure for Humans
[thread] 👇

Moving boxes: Keep an inventory table of what you put in each box 📦 when you move.

I once lost the box of all my favorite books from childhood and will never remember some of the titles 😭.

You can do something similar for tracking what data is stored on external hard drives. https://t.co/UaWYByjsKA

Change of address: If you're like me and you move a lot, maintain a table of all the services you have to notify when you move.

In the US, you can use USPS's mail forwarding to catch the ones you may have forgotten... https://t.co/thDvnau25h

Apartment hunting: It's awful. Tables make it a little easier. Write down the address, showing times, lease conditions, and your thoughts.

Keep video tours and images for each entry so you remember which is which.

NYC y u so expensive 😩 https://t.co/Ljle26WkTZ

How many days have you taken off in the last year? A table remembers.

Friends borrow your stuff? Don't depend on them to remember.

What about loyalty programs and cancelled reservation credit? There's a table for that. https://t.co/rCQb4lhQYM

Goals: Put goals in a table along with concrete outcomes and deadlines.

Then you will know whether you've succeeded and can reflect on where to improve. 

Inspiration: @fortelabs's https://t.co/q2SF2ySjvT https://t.co/uVssRjkWAY

Predict the future: You want to be right 75% of the time when you're 75% sure. Use tables to track your uncertainty and calibrate yourself.

@NotionHQ & @airtable templates: https://t.co/AdkSeBTPh3 https://t.co/NLoGW7ZHTp

Country notes: Whenever you hear something about a country that sounds exciting, add it to a table.

Makes travel-planning much easier. https://t.co/GFwGVL67hG

After I'm Gone: Make it easier on your friends, in the unlikely event something horrible happens to you. 

Whoever's going to be inheriting your money and planning your funeral should know how to access your money and who to contact, respectively. https://t.co/XKklrZqNAa

Use Queues 🙋 to send things to your future self. It's like a time capsule that never gets sealed.

Data Structures for Humans
[thread] 👇

For all your friends you don't see regularly, keep a queue of things to talk about.

That way, you don't have to interrupt them often and you always have something to say. https://t.co/mkIqIWcALh

Media to Consume: One of the biggest queues I have is a queue of which movies, TV, books, games, and art to check out when I've got more time.

* Goodreads shelves + sorting
* Apps like @instapaper, @Pocket, and @readwiseio
* A "read next" browser bookmark folder https://t.co/CPbMCRv50f

Task inbox: Use queues as a future inbox.

Doing housework but want to write about something? Put it in a "notes" queue for later.

A task you only do in batches of 6 items or more? Put it in a queue and wait for 5 more.

Relevant: https://t.co/PFWVZmeH6a https://t.co/u0djj8O75b

Use Literate Checklists ☑ for recurring experiences where the checklist actually helps you complete the tasks.

Data Structures for Humans
[thread] 👇

Pretend you've written the literate checklist for someone else. Tell them *what* you want them to do AND *how*.

Add links to items that are online tasks.

Embed code to do the work. Both quick stuff and long running tasks. Human authority + computer automation 🤵🏽🤝🤖. https://t.co/2boSS5cW5b

Time checklist: A literate checklist for each time granularity.

Every week, review. Every month, course correct. Every year, re-imagine who you want to be.

I also like to make a time capsule video every 3 months and send a personal newsletter to friends every 6. https://t.co/pTN4FyNIwu

Event checklist: A literate checklist at the beginning or end of something.

Going on travel? Starting/ending a project? Reading a new book? Making a major decision? Planning a party? Writing a new blog post? Getting in flow? Make a checklist.

Add good ideas as you think of them https://t.co/n6BEy0Znpf

When going somewhere, I used to think the best way to deal with other walkers/joggers/bikers/drivers was:

Don’t inconvenience anyone. Stay in your “lane”. Make your actions obvious and predictable.

But walking and riding around Taipei all this year, I’ve prioritized a new strategy:

If you have more information about the situation, then you are more responsible for managing it.

Ex: people can’t see backwards. If you are biking behind them, it is up to YOU not to bump them, even if they are blocking you.

Before, I would just get mad that they are inconsiderate. But now, I get to strategize. It’s a game of skill and communication.

There’s something deeper here I think. Like a willingness to meet people where they’re at instead of holding them up to unreasonable standards.

Give love, not shame.

Righteous indignation... seems like a core American emotion. One that we express to others a lot.

Living in Taiwan this year, I've noticed people don't bring it up often. And each time I feel it now, it seems petty.

Why's it so prevalent in the US? Pros/cons?
I could just say it's better not to have righteous indignation, but that's just my culture shock "honeymoon" phase talking--where one naively thinks everything's better in the country they moved to.

No, there's definitely something cool about righteous indignation.

It's sort of like a strong feedback mechanism. Did someone in the government/your town/your company/your family do something that went too far? Then you can signal that with righteous indignation.

Just as we expect supply/demand in a decent market to price the goods well, so too do we expect authority/righteous indignation in a decent society to balance personal/common needs.

Something like that 🤷‍♀️.

I'm becoming a game developer. So here's a thread of my practicing random stuff.

In the future, I hope to look back on these and cringe laugh at novice they are.

#1 Health Bar 1

Homework assignment from @FreyaHolmer's https://t.co/rJc2c7ogBI

* Health < 20%, color is red
* 20% < Health < 80%, color transitions red to green
* 80% < Health, color is green https://t.co/WqirOi1BVt

#2 Health Bar 2

Homework assignment from @FreyaHolmer's https://t.co/rJc2c7ogBI

* Gets color from an image texture
* Health < 20%, blinks red
* The round edges and border are created using Signed Distance Fields, i.e. clever math can create precise shapes https://t.co/ktioYWzBDw

#3 Walking Dust

Trying to recreate @AggroCrabGames's walking dust effect from Going Under (this game looks so awesome!). https://t.co/7F7xcGAaXZ
Here's the original https://t.co/nshur5lDbo

#4 Attack Explosion

Recreating 
@AggroCrabGames's guitar attack effect from Going Under.

This refracting bubble is basically... the closer a pixel is to the center of the bubble, the closer it will sample from the center of the stuff behind/inside the bubble (used Fresnel). https://t.co/4gmWOiceX6
Here's the original https://t.co/epoLQtZiUH

#5 Fire

Recreating 
@AggroCrabGames's torches in Going Under.

A combination of procedurally generated clouds (i.e. noise) moving upwards and cleverly combining them with a circle. https://t.co/kzFG4RLwfb
Here's the original https://t.co/MEvezF39Kv

#6 Dark Sky

Recreating an effect I saw on Twitter https://t.co/n1Vqu8c9mg

* Stars are voronoi noise with high contrast
* Rings are distance from center put through sin function
* Hills are actual 3D object https://t.co/uXHjizMKIT

Here's a snapshot of what the process looks like for making the stars.

It starts off as this weird looking voronoi cellular thing,  but change the scale and apply more contrast and you've got stars! https://t.co/5gYsz11idd

#7 Attack Flash

Recreating @AggroCrabGames's attack flash effect in Going Under.

This one could still use some work. It's 3 oblong particles emitted at the same time, turning to blue and fading out before dying. https://t.co/hdzWwMSYjq
Here's the original https://t.co/4KR0gZtAK0

#8 Charging Arrows

Recreating @AggroCrabGames's Caffiend charging arrows in Going Under.

* Made an image of an arrow with bars on the side
* Tiled arrow image vertically
* Clever use of inverse lerp function stretches edges of image so I can add/remove space between arrows https://t.co/7VrVLdKDm7
Original https://t.co/ukyepzuYus

#9 Stream Waves

Recreating @PlayUnexplored's stream waves (this game looks dope BTW).

Emits particles that leave trails as they move. Left-right motion and size fluctuate along each particle's lifetime. https://t.co/tbChgjRvyG
Original from the game https://t.co/oITp6cNCWb

#10 Enemy Sight

Homework assignment from 
@FreyaHolmer's https://t.co/B3JOcUacXb

* Red = enemy sees player. Green = not seen
* Had to use the dot product for this assignment, so

enemy sight vector ⋅ enemy to player vector

helps us calculate if player is seen or not. https://t.co/GlL4MTZmaP

. @FreyaHolmer also made this cool visualization showing the dot product between two normalized vectors.

https://t.co/bib7B345Sh

#11 Crippling Swirl

Recreating @larianstudios's "Crippling Blow" swirl effect in Divinity: Original Sin 2. https://t.co/lCD0QB22HV
Original https://t.co/HTK5BFhIkn

It's common to pass an always-increasing value (e.g. the current time) to the sine function to create the illusion of constant movement.

On the left, whiter = further from the center. Then we add the current time, multiply by ~how many loops we want, and pass to sine. https://t.co/op4wX1wz1i
Here's the same thing, only the input is a side-to-side/linear gradient instead of a radial one. https://t.co/k6LjYEemCs

#12 Kaleidoscope

Recreating my own kaleidoscope effect from the "Spiral" section https://t.co/zMufDUN46q, but this time using Unity instead of the SVG image library.

Again, thanks Lucy Nieto for sharing this psychedelic image:
https://t.co/BREFw0Ilrx https://t.co/4A58QuD5Mz

The Unity graph for creating this ended up being not that sexy and kind of tedious.

I created a template "slice" for representing a single slice at a starting rotation and then added it together 8 times. https://t.co/Ftptkvbyhj

The most interesting part was creating the "slice" mask. 

To create a line at a 45° angle, you can get all the points where the y coordinate is greater than the x coordinate. So e.g. (5, 0) doesn't work/is black, but (0, 5) works/is white. https://t.co/yCouVv04C0

#13 Fire and Lightning Petals

Recreating @ShannonBerke's clever flower idea:
https://t.co/vhceXJ9dj8

* The effect is spherical because the 3D object is a sphere
* The sphere's surface (UVs) unwraps like a world map, so it's easy  to animate a flat image from top to bottom. https://t.co/TqnylQ8ZZX

I built-in a bit of customization.

It probably makes more sense to work in tandem with an artist to create images (e.g. fire, water, etc) with good transparency from the get-go. The images I chose are not super appropriate for this use case. https://t.co/qw3bKM83O9

#14 Zippy Force Shield

Uses the same structure as #13, except the 3D object is a cube. https://t.co/8G21nd4w8f

#15 Music in the Heart

Inspired by @PeerPlay's tutorial https://t.co/4OmsPBRW4T

* Low frequencies have more data, so the bars on the left represent fewer frequencies to spread it out more
* Exponentially weighted moving average smooths vertical changes a little (but not a lot) https://t.co/bSczb91mov

Visualizing sound is *really* hard.

I thought it'd be hard to turn the audio into frequency data, but the real challenge is knowing the shape of your specific music and the idiosyncrasies of the human ear.

This GIF is

y = log(a * freq_value(x))

for some log base and some 'a' https://t.co/HWs4toU1hG

#18 CPU vs GPU

These look the same, but they're not.
* Left uses CPU "for loop" to part-by-part calculate the shape of the object every frame
* Right uses GPU "shader" to calculate many parts of the shape in parallel at the same time

#23 The Room's Spinning

A simple brick texture. Very inspired by a certain old Windows screensaver:
https://t.co/95ZYHXZYlv https://t.co/lpSMZaGho0
This brick texture starts with what all good 3D shaders start with... a sine wave. Black... white... black... white... https://t.co/Uqn5jThqEI

Next... coordinates.

We use a "tiling" node to create the values 0 to N in the X and Y direction.

Then "modulus" so 0 to N becomes 0 to 1 then again 0 to 1 over and over N times. That'll end up being N rows of bricks.

Finally, add the sine wave to create movement. https://t.co/dFZMKL3RzY
In each of these little squares created in the previous part, we shift only the top half over by adding 0.5 so it looks more like bricks. https://t.co/bAulRdXGkU
The last step is mostly using a "mask" for both the "brick" color and "mortar" color and combining them together. https://t.co/PN5wRPlJc5

#45 Wave Function is Collapsing

A maze generator like my previous one, only this uses Wave Function Collapse, a technique to generate scenery by only placing things next to each other that should be adjacent e.g. a doorway and walls on either side.

https://t.co/PYUCpUPqLW https://t.co/1MzUWqY2Yr

I used @boris_brave's Tessera in Unity (thanks @benhhopkins).

1) Create tiles that the algorithm can put on the map
2) Use color to indicate which tiles can be adjacent

Then, during generation, if the next tile to place is not obvious, ~randomly choose one that fits. https://t.co/g6A9os9rqu

Let's imagine you're writing the code in your game for a conveyor belt.

Pretty straightforward right? Just walk down your list of items from front to back and move them along every frame in your game. https://t.co/PFmeUWyJ9M

What about this?

Hmm, ok maybe time to structure your data more like a graph? Walk from the front of your conveyor belt(s) backwards using breadth-first-search, moving if there's room.

At the intersection of belts, there are several options. Just choose one. Not too too crazy… https://t.co/OTfYpQ8kwp

Hmm 😵‍💫… this can be represented as a graph, but now it's not clear where the "front" is.

When you close the loop, what do you set as the front of the belt? When you create a 3-way or 4-way intersection, how do you know if that's closing a loop started elsewhere or not? https://t.co/hFm02sx4vR

You could just try to move every item on the belt every frame and hope that clears it up (it will).

But then you your items will move like an accordion: how can you guarantee, on each frame, you will move that item before the open space first?

Should these be moving? Maybe it's not worth it? But what if they hold different kinds of items?

Loops are hard. I am working on a factory game and struggling to come up with a unified solution.

#53 Belts with loops

I am grateful to this post by Atahan that helped a lot:
https://t.co/XJdSvePxnD

Two phases:
1. Try to move objects in order from furthest from the start (for loops, start from anywhere) and move what you can
2. Anything that couldn't move, try to move again https://t.co/kn4i24pmmP

^ this is based on some observations:
1. A resource can only move a tile's width on each turn
2. By freeing up space at the boundary where the path loops into itself (phase #1), you can move something across that boundary (phase #2).

I'm still not completely sure this works, but I've done quite a scenarios out on paper and it always works, even with paths splitting and merging.

This was my first attempt that ultimately failed 🤦‍♀️ https://t.co/L6mb2XIolT

I've come up with a tentative solution to this problem. Every time the player adds or removes a belt, I create an ordering (topologically), so belts always run before those behind them.

Then, on each frame, I use this ordering and two phase algorithm.

The software engineer will say an algorithm full of if-else chains, embedded functions, and map lookups couldn't possibly affect performance because it's constant time.

The software engineer need only attach the profiler to know where the time's spent.

#61 Slither Slithering Along

I'm participating in a 10 day game jam https://t.co/iSG7Dpw8a4. The game I'm working on is somewhat inspired by the carrying mechanics in Death Stranding, with silly characteristics. 

Model by @benhhopkins 🫰 https://t.co/ih7YM0j6LK

There will be different "harnesses" sticking out of Glube (a gelatinous cube) that enable you to transport cargo around.

In Unity, the harnesses are attached via a "hinge joint" with a spring, so they can rotate on an axis, but also are pulled back towards a starting position. https://t.co/zMejpqNLXW

I originally was sending out raycasts from each corner of Glube's bottom to try to create some kind of hovercraft.

But it turned out to be easier to just slide Glube along the floor by averaging the height of the terrain below and moving/angling Glube appropriately. https://t.co/C2D1F9zSQu
Leaning and jumping have been hard to implement because I've been trying to keep the cargo heavy so they don't fall out so easily, but then everything gets out of whack. https://t.co/RSQbfap5KE

Inspired by others, I'm going to try using a rapid-prototyping approach to game design and document the process.

My plan is to make more and more complicated prototypes of a few game ideas until one of them seems like the clear best.

So far, I've got about 5 game ideas...

1. Topdown, co-op multiplayer, crafting. You start with nothing and must mine resources from nature to craft machines to catch other resources that fall to Earth on a time schedule.  

Inspired by games like Overcooked, Unrailed! (below), Lovers In A Dangerous Spacetime https://t.co/e1hzA4tD3q

2. One player, platformer. Dark level except for a shining ghost to guide you across treacherous landscape. The ghost is another player's recording going through the level, passing on their knowledge to you.

Inspired by Nintendo's Mario platforming (below), Death Stranding. https://t.co/Sa8IAHs5Ba

3. One player, topdown, squad. Make your way across a spaceship graveyard, performing SWAT-like assaults on spaceships. However, you must time your actions with the recorded actions of another player. Air and bullets are scarce.

Inspired by Door Kickers (below), Death Stranding https://t.co/f97Wwn84ad

4. Couch multiplayer, arcade. Big Chinese characters fall down onto the map. You must avoid getting squished. Each character will summon a different hazard, based on its meaning: 牛 (cow) spawns bulls, 雞 (chicken) spawns eggs, etc.

Inspired by Mario Party's Book Squirm (below) https://t.co/KSdpS1egr3

5. 1st/3rd person, stealth. Use your camera's flash to temporarily scare off predators or the film to teleport to wherever you're looking. But after you teleport, you must look for and take another player's teleport path to refill your film.

Inspired by Death Stranding. https://t.co/Ghj2y5Ik95

I’ve been living in Taiwan ~3 years. People here are very practical. Function over form.
The versatility of bricks. https://t.co/jdPPd5i9FT
Homes, businesses, even city workers will use every day objects to patch things together, whereas I’m used to people using specialized objects for each purpose.
String holds things in place https://t.co/Sij5cXuG8E

More generally, my sense is that people don’t care for the crafted “experiences” from products, services, restaurants, etc. like westerners.

On Airbnb you can literally purchase experiences.

Certainly there are loads of exceptions. The Taiwanese have invested lots into shaping everyday experiences with clothing, decoration, aesthetic, …

But it’s one of the most striking differences with my American upbringing.
Containers can hold things up (an oil tank for plants, a vase used to make a cement weight) https://t.co/W2kGj2VUzC
It stands out to me because the objects don’t match aesthetically. It’s a bit of an eyesore, if I’m being honest.

But, since I’m being honest, it actually matches my personality more.

I’ve been hanging on the coattails of people with good taste. It’d be a world of hiking shoes, fanny packs, and big clothes with a thousand pockets if everyone was like me.
A drying rack? https://t.co/pRm5UNo3rv

Repurposing everyday objects to meet the day’s challenges is great!

Enriching the story of your life by creating experiences is great too.

You can have both.

@DreadCanary The cities in Taiwan are just “tai” with a direction added:

Taichung = tai middle
Tainan = tai south
Taitung = tai east

My favorite game to play is “Why’s that thing the way it is?”

1. Pick a thing e.g. building, family, video, experience
2. Ask why it is the way it is. Wrong answers welcome

I am always playing 100s of variations of this game in my head and with friends in conversation

Examples

What does the waiter think about what I just said?

Why do people like hot water but not hot air?

What’d happen if we couldn’t tie knots?

Why are we always using floor tiling?

JessCamp 2 thoughts thread

1. Always thought I needed to live in a city, but maybe it's more like good friends, walkability, AC/heat, good internet, super market, a few restaurants? Or maybe it's a reaction to growing up in an American suburb?
2. Maybe I should be in Europe? I forgot how gorgeous the aesthetic can be. When I was walking around the small German town, my mind was constantly 🤯ing OMG THAT'S BEAUTIFUL

3. A prominent part of me is afraid of woo, anti-scientism, spiritual/religious belief, conspiracy theorizing, etc.

My love for other people and curiosity keep it from boiling over, but I'd rather my self be aligned better

4. An online persona is just one dimension and may show up differently--or not at all--when meeting in person.

A nice laugh or smile is enough for me to fall in friendlove.

5. Perceived status serves a purpose but is an obstacle for me when interacting in person.

At VibeCamp, people often pointed "high status accounts" out to me and it made it hard to be myself around them ewww. I went in blind to JessCamp and it was so much more fun 💃

6. Drugs are good mkay?

More concretely, why did I wait 16 years to do psychedelics (and co.) again?--oops should this be on alt?

7. I think this is the first time I didn't flinch taking off my shirt (to go swimming) around strangers.

I was about 375lb (170kg) when I started university and am working through deeply entrenched body issues, even though I've dropped about half that weight since.

8. People were so generous with their things and time and effort! There was an abundance mindset and this is first time I've felt like a place came close to the integrity I've in the monastery. I never worried someone would steal anything.

9. First time cuddling with non-queer people. At first, I worried that they'd be weirded out--the men because I'm queer and the women because I'm a cis man. But it was so good and I hope to do it again.

10. I was listening to someone play guitar and it was a special song for me. I started crying hard. The towo people next to me held me but I didn't want to hide my tears... I wanted people to see and know the overwhelming love I felt and to normalize being emotional in public.
11. I joined some impromptu musical band moments, usually as percussionist, and it was amazing! I love being part of that. I want to make music with other people again!!!

12. A circling session years ago taught me that I cling hard to acting diplomatic. More of my personality came out this week when I let go of that intention a few times and I enjoyed the company of several people who didn't embody this sentiment at all 😂

13. Some people were intrigued by my living in Taiwan. I felt embarrassed sometimes, feeling like I was leaning on that to be interesting... but also fuck that I put 1000s of hours into learning Chinese and making a living in a "strange land"--I'm fucking incredible.
@bluueflowerrr My country loyalty can be bought. What’s your starting bid? 😜

14. Being in water (with friends?) is so joyous

A close friend used to guide me through difficult feelings by asking me to first think of a safe place and mine was a deep, shady hole filled with water.

Let's go sit in the river again 🌊 https://t.co/iszQ9COYIc

15. State your needs and you may just get it (via @frideswyth).

There were always people offering things: charging cables, herb, food, hugs, money, ... Others said Burning Man had this vibe too--hope to check it out soon :)

Also relates to good poly and connection in general.

Did a psilocybin/shrooms (3g) thing yesterday. Still journaling and processing. The most clear change is now I’m sure I won’t be insane forever if I do it again.

Some scattered memories below 💈
It was a therapy environment, but almost the entire experience was nonverbal, with an eye mask on.
I derive myself from first principles, I said as the shrooms started to kick hard.

It’s possible you will forget where you are, he told me. I didn’t forget that fact, but once my brain started to melt in the peak, I lost track that I was alive. Was I was an astral entity that lived in this strange world I’d dreamt up? I was completely detached.

When I started to come down, I remember thinking that everything was just utterly absurd: death, internal organs, evolution, foreign languages, sex, the US, New York, … there’s no way that’s all real!

I could imagine many concepts, but felt unable to remember their entirety. The “chunking” in my brain wasn’t working anymore. I knew I was queer, but couldn’t remember what that really meant. Sexuality, what an absurd idea!

Did I really just fly from Europe? Do I really live in Taiwan or did I make that up? Who is this therapist and how did I get here?

Although I forgot who I  was, throughout the experience, I was anchored on my partner’s name. Never forgot.
My past memories just felt like a slurry of gooey things that happened to someone… to me? I hardly know the guy. Had I been on this trip since I was in Europe? Had I been on this trip my whole life?
Moments of letting go of fear of death. Appreciation for my therapist for holding the space and love in general for the kindness of people for one another.
I am definitely insane, I told the therapist. He laughed and reassured me that I was just on psychedelics. But no, I was definitely insane—I was sure of it.

I’ve been here before, I told myself several times. It felt like I had asked a question in my last trip in 2007 and then it’d been buried by layers of life’s minutiae. But what was the question? It never materialized 😕 I’ll have to go back again to remember.
@the_wilderless A site map!

Oh! The power of a good story...

I used to think that obscuring reality with story was an exploitation only perpetrated by marketers and government propagandists.

But now I realize telling a good story is just a tool and it is probably the most wonderful tool one can wield.

My happiness seems to come from the story that I tell. There are the parts of reality that are hard to change, but meaning/significance are extremely malleable. 

Working from a cafe, getting closure, therapy, brainstorming ... the story is everything!
https://t.co/9atuGt6nXM

I discovered years ago that you can have a new thought and enhance your story by basically spinning a roulette wheel in your brain.
https://t.co/zX9LQgVVsn

This line of thinking's made me think more about my gamedev work. Gamification of life experiences is something we should be pouring, dumping time and money into.

For me, the difference between an interest and a career is just how compelling the story is.

"Every summer, those coming-of-age must make a pilgrimage across the land on foot, starting at the First City--the cradle of civilization--and converging on the capital. Groups of four must either walk along the cities, the outer shrines, or the rivers, rocks, and forests."

Discussing reframing parts of life as adventure with @tasshinfogleman got me thinking about this project, "Adventure Calls", that I never finished.

In 2021, I made several Speculative Design projects, design that invites people to talk about future issues, today.

Fiction has seriously influenced my thoughts and feelings throughout life, so it seemed natural to me that a type of design steeped in fiction but rooted in reality could have a huge impact on society.

Instead of preaching about what should be done differently, you can just design a product, a building, a scene, a world where it's already happening. If it resonates, cool. If not, no problem. You don't have to wait for reality to catch up to your dream.

The yearning to play and call to adventure never goes away. In "Adventure Calls", I was making a fictional government brochure that informs young people about the national annual pilgrimage, with fake photographs and testimonials.

God, I want this energy in everyday life!

"St. Sentimental's Foster Home for Adults" was my attempt to create a place that provides the care you'd experience growing up in a loving family, but for adults.
https://t.co/G6891lXRs9

I never had to think about how much it would cost, nor if it was even possible. https://t.co/cGpYWKqlWj

Check out @5putniko's work if you're interested in learning more about Speculative/Critical Design
https://t.co/V4N2W6Wh2I

Just finished an ayahuasca retreat. Integrating a lot… might write more later.

My biggest takeaway is that my beliefs and stories I indulge in don’t need to match my feelings/body sensations. I can still be optimistic while I’m steeping in overwhelming despair and nausea.

It’s something I already believed intellectually, but it became my lifeline 🛟 When my emotions and cognitive fog wanted me to believe that I’d always been deeply unhappy and always will be, there was this voice that was like nah man, that’s just a story… life’s pretty good, no?

Bringing it into my everyday experience, it shows up like “Ughh I’m exhausted, but that doesn’t mean this person I just met isn’t cool” or “I’m sad that my friend didn’t thank me for that favor I did, but that doesn’t mean my current projects are hopeless”

Once upon a time, a brave group of children decided they wanted to face the things that scared them the most so they could grow into secure, loving adults.

In a small cabin, they summoned a portal. Each child saw different things... things they were too afraid to even think.

One child in particular saw a burning cornfield inhabited by a colossal demon on its back. The demon slowly pulled mommy and daddy and the other children towards its mouth. Dreams and aspirations alike were swallowed whole.

Overwhelmed, the child fled the room.

"Wait!" the other children pleaded, but the child ran all the way home, locked the door, and hid under the blankets.

Hours later, a knock at the door. The demon? Another knock. Little voices too. Out from the blankets the child crept and slowly opened the door.

"Friends! I thought you were facing your fears."
"We were but... not without you." 
"No," the child lowered their head, "I can't do it."
"You can! We'll find a way... we'll go slow and easy."
"Are you sure?"
"Of course! We love you 🥹 We choose you."

The End
@cedar_xr @knuffx Yaaaa storybooks are beautiful 😊

Here are some scattered thoughts about a recent ayahuasca retreat I attended 🍵

We did four ceremonies in five nights and it was probably the most intense experience of my life.
1 The container was really special. The tradition of this place was from Peru. We started by meeting in the cabin at sundown. Various herbs were burned in different ways for protection.

2 A single candle lit the room. The facilitators offered each person a cup of the medicine, encouraging us to share our intention for the ceremony. Mine were simple like "Don't fight", "Have fun", ...
Then the candle was put out for the ceremony. Only about 90 minutes in, they briefly relit it, offering everyone a second cup of the medicine. Otherwise, the ceremony was completely dark.

3 I spent a lot of time in despair, but after almost three full ceremonies of that, I'd developed a new frame of mind that helped me from drowning 
https://t.co/6ICk6T5fFT

In despair, I thought about my aging, my failed aspirations, my death, my parents' deaths, growing apart from my college friends, not wanting to marry my current lover, my stalled career, climate change, the heat death of the universe, ...

"That's all just concepts!" I started saying, noticing a lot of "shoulds" come up. "You experience a ceremony like this." Fuck you, that's a concept, I'll do it the way I do it. Wait, that's a concept too, needing to be so individualistic. Recursive threads like this in my head

4 I developed a lot of compassion though. Hearing other people in the dark go through hell sobbing, burping, vomiting into buckets (most do at some point), yawning, swallowing... I couldn't help but cradle them in my heart during their bare vulnerability
Even weeks later, hearing others' bodily noises... I can't help but appreciate them. Haha wut brains are silly I love you.

5 Our facilitators were astoundingly good musicians and played acoustic instruments and sang throughout. Best concerts I ever attended, yet I was miserable most of the time.

The music was similar to this
https://t.co/DI2YJmcd7m
"It's dark out here, but it's nice to have a friend" was a phrase I came up with to describe some of the music. A feeling of fearful exploration into the unknown, but fleeting hints of hope.

There were also songs that made many people purge (vomit). As the song started, I'd feel my throat lubricate as I felt more nauseous in expectation and then the person next to me would swallow, feeling it too.

Music like this sometimes made me writhe.
https://t.co/ucd3mdr1VP

6 I hallucinated a lot about bugs, especially ants and spiders. Not terribly comfortable. Several times, the sound of rustling leaves, smell of tobacco smoke, and my raised body temperature made me feel like we were all insects trapped and burning in a pile of dry brush.

7 In a similar way, I surfaced a deep fear of mine: Fear of The Struggle... of having no power, no means, being trapped, without comfort, doing slave labor in an oppressive system until I die.

A manifestation of that is probably not wanting to get stuck in a 9-5 job :/

8 Each ceremony, I wanted to give up. "After this ceremony," I told myself, "I will tell them I quit. It's too much!"

I felt like everyone else was braver than me. Out of desperate self-compassion, I invented this story, The Child That Stepped Into Hell 
https://t.co/qpCwVOHMRU

In one moment of especially strong self-love, I started asking "Why not me?" Why don't I get to be happy? I'm handsome, smart, creative, funny, kind, hard working... Why not me? It was a huge emotional release.

9 On the last night, there was a moment when I could see how ayahuasca ceremonies might have started. Clapping along with the music, I imagined it'd be fun to sit together with the people you love, take plant medicine, cry, purge, and scream together. Very intimate.

The quality of the experience was so different than my psilocybin trip earlier this summer, but so many of the same feelings came up...

I don't regret attending, but it may be a long time before I try ayahuasca again
https://t.co/NcZOQbMBKc

A belief I adopted this summer was that if I take a big enough dose of psychedelics, there’s a good chance I’ll get over my fear of death and feelings of being worthless, and the only obstacle is my fear to do so.

Is there any truth to this? Have I gone insane? Will I go insane?

When I say it out loud, it sounds so crazy.

But it’s definitely something I partially believe. Like there’s definitely a part of me that’s like when I find a safe container I’m just gonna eat a pile of mushrooms and hold on for dear life.

It has a “naive easy solution to a hard problem” smell, but also the biggest changes in my life have usually been under normal circumstances. Maybe eating the mushrooms is some kind of submission, accepting whatever the medicine brings. And that took years to get to.

@LexerLux I know what you mean. There are different scopes and I’ve tended to stay higher level because it’s easier.

I aspire to talk more about specifics like balancing, timing, enemy choices, character movement, etc. It’s been hard to develop an analytical intuition for that though.

I’ve got this process now where I’ll record myself playing a game for an hour or so, then review the footage in my video editor at 4x/8x speed, taking notes on what mechanics I think contributed to my feelings during that gameplay and adding markers directly on the video files. https://t.co/QsYrIknMfP

When I write up the script for a game design video, it feels a lot like what @fortelabs and  @david_perell always talk about re: intermediate packets and snapping legos together. Like I just look at all my notes for a particular game, group them into patterns, then write it.

Earlier, I looked online for tips on improving game design. People said to analyze other people’s games. At the time, I was like I dunno sounds like so much work.

And now, having a creative outlet for it makes it feel so rewarding. I love thinking about other people’s games 🙂

What changed for me was realizing that I can just start from fundamentals: what do I feel, what about the game makes me feel that way, and what choice did the game designer make that produces that?

This is what many game design books preach and it’s totally on the money.

Originally, I kinda thought I had to study a bunch of theory. That definitely helps too. And designing/developing your own games helps a lot.

But really, every gamer’s feelings are valid 🥹 I just need to start from there. Nobody can tell me how I *should* feel about art.

i've written off learning a lot of stuff in life, not because it was hard, but because i wasn't willing to learn it piecemeal, going deeper into the rabbit hole each time

but darling, u can learn it. we have the technology.

what's not called upon in everyday life disappears, so the most successful strategy for me so far is just making it present in everyday life

my favorite way to do that is to make it social, committing to fun stuff with others that call upon the knowledge (projects, games, class)

thoughts on jesscamp 3

1. meeting people during the pre-party, it felt like a wonderful family reunion. i didn’t realize how much i loved being us

2. i found out the day before the event that the cold i had the week before was actually covid. the discussion about how the community should deal with covid was hard. a non-trivial number of people got sick in the end. still thinking about this, the tradeoffs, consent/boundaries

3. one of my biggest regrets is comparing the experience to last year. jc2 was really special but it was my first time, so everything was new (i stole this observation from someone). i think us jc2 people romanticized jc2 and created unrealistic expectations for all. i’m sorry

4. the ~100 of us were split across 5 lodges. cooking was volunteer-based. lots of great socializing in the kitchens, the carnivores cooking in the vegan lodge, pairing the sauce from omnivore lodge with chana masala from vegetarian lodge

5. satanic rituals in the woods a.k.a. collaborative dancing, singing, cooing, growling in the midnight fields. if u can’t make out anyone’s face, then u can be performative af and it’s all given to the collective
6. i got to show off all the clothes i bought in the last year and got many compliments 😋 https://t.co/eVSrgjSjCa
7. there was no day where everyone took [redacted] and that was fine. look forward to doing it in the future but it’s not necessary to cultivating feelings of love for others
8. felt like i was less aware of status. i tried not to ask anyone who they were on here (…and now i’m struggling to add everyone i met 😅). it feels like an authenticity hack
9. only a few weeks ago i asked for advice on how to love dancing. @RichDecibels got me to go to @not_a_hot_girl’s dj set at jc3 and the cringe dance maxxing was so liberating https://t.co/96S6qDrFHD

Jhourney retreat thread

a retreat to help people experience jhana. most of the sits, u just do on my own, in your own home. there'd be guided meditations together on zoom each morning and evening and sometimes during the day https://t.co/WkZM1WRtwx

the focus on just relaxing was such a nice foundation

at various points, they encouraged us all to take advantage of the container and sit for many hours. but it didn't feel like other retreats. "if it feels like work or effort, just drop that and relax"

my compulsion to police my mind during the retreat was self-inflicted, not top-down. it took the whole week to quiet that harmful voice in my head
https://t.co/1Cjc7wqdEG

there was lots of boredom and frustration. i had 3 huge emotional releases during the retreat, probably because i was going through a breakup, and probably because i am so cruel to myself that just a little forgiveness meditation was enough to clear years of self-inflicted shame

i forgive you for not meeting your own expectations and i forgive you for having unrealistic expectations

(while walking around my hometown) i forgive that other person for not living up to the weird and strict expectations that i just made up right this second

i felt a strong connection to the ocean during my sits:

i am the ocean
i am the waves
i am the rain
(i am the water)
i am the breath

these mantras brought me a lot of joy:

"may ulysse have these memories (of his partner)... always in his heart"
"may ulysse be happy. may ulysse be forgiven"
"may ulysse have these feelings. thank you for these feelings"

i even wrote a poem to myself

i forgive U for not being proud of yourself
i forgive U for not receiving your love and their love
i forgive U for not doing what U want to do, and being the way U want to be
i forgive U for giving a shit about how u dance
i forgive U for giving a…

i thought, surely since i'm going through a breakup and totally changed my diet and was super horny and not meditating 10 hour a day, there was no way to enter jhana for me

but on the last full retreat day, at the end of the evening meditation, i found jhana 1

i'd imagined my breath as the waves at the beach the whole retreat. during this particular sit, i imagined that every out-breath was me playfully "throwing water" at my legs

and then i felt it on the tip of my brain. fun! "remember fun?" i could feel how close i was to pure fun

and then this feeling of fun that'd been creating a pleasing feeling in my legs spread throughout my body. "is this jhana 🦋?" i asked myself for the thousandth time. oh. shit. this must be it. the good feelings bubbled up and just kept surging from the top of my head. for 45 min

as the facilitators wrapped up the evening sit talking about logistics, i was just grinning like an idiot. it was a little hard to concentrate because i was so curious about what was going on in my head https://t.co/AZGCxqtGx0

coming out of the retreat, i decided that i like (this kind of) meditation. i look forward to relaxing a lot more and cultivating enjoyment and being curious about my experience. thank u to the folks at @jhanatech
